title: Security+ SY0-701 — Section 13: Cryptography & PKI
description: Jason Dion Security+ SY0-701 — Section 13: Cryptography and Public Key Infrastructure. Domain: General Security Concepts (1.0).

questions.question: What is the key difference between symmetric and asymmetric encryption?
answers: Symmetric uses one shared key; asymmetric uses a public/private key pair
Symmetric is slower; asymmetric is faster
Symmetric uses two keys; asymmetric uses one
Symmetric is only used for hashing
questions.explanation: Symmetric encryption uses a single shared secret key for both encryption and decryption (fast, used for bulk data). Asymmetric uses a key pair — public key encrypts, private key decrypts (slower, used for key exchange and digital signatures).

questions.question: What is AES and why is it the preferred symmetric encryption standard?
answers: Advanced Encryption Standard — fast, secure, supports 128/192/256-bit keys, NIST-approved
A type of asymmetric encryption for key exchange
An older encryption standard replaced by DES
A hashing algorithm for password storage
questions.explanation: AES is the current NIST standard symmetric cipher. It is extremely fast in hardware and software, supports multiple key lengths, and has no known practical attacks against it.

questions.question: What is RSA used for in cryptography?
answers: Asymmetric encryption for key exchange and digital signatures
Hashing passwords for secure storage
Encrypting large files quickly
Generating symmetric session keys
questions.explanation: RSA is an asymmetric algorithm used for encrypting small data (key exchange), digital signatures, and certificates. It is too slow for bulk encryption of large data.

questions.question: What is a cryptographic hash function used for?
answers: Producing a fixed-size digest to verify data integrity — one-way, non-reversible
Encrypting data for secure transmission
Generating encryption keys
Authenticating users to a system
questions.explanation: Hash functions (SHA-256, SHA-3) produce a fixed-length digest. They are one-way (cannot reverse the hash to get original data) and used to verify integrity (file downloads, passwords, digital signatures).

questions.question: What is a salt in password hashing?
answers: A random value added to a password before hashing to prevent rainbow table attacks
An extra character appended to passwords
An encryption key derived from the password
A second hash function applied after the first
questions.explanation: A salt is a unique random value added to each password before hashing. It ensures two identical passwords produce different hashes, defeating precomputed rainbow table attacks.

questions.question: How does a digital signature provide non-repudiation?
answers: The sender signs with their private key — only they have it, proving they authored the message
The recipient signs with a shared key to confirm receipt
Both parties sign with the same key
A trusted third party signs on behalf of the sender
questions.explanation: A digital signature is created by encrypting a hash with the sender's private key. Since only the sender has the private key, the signature proves authorship and prevents denial (non-repudiation).

questions.question: What does a digital certificate contain?
answers: The subject's public key, identity information, CA signature, and validity period
The subject's private key and password
Only the subject's name and email
A symmetric encryption key for secure communication
questions.explanation: An X.509 digital certificate binds a public key to an identity, signed by a Certificate Authority. It contains the public key, owner info, issuer, serial number, and expiration date.

questions.question: What is a Certificate Authority (CA)?
answers: A trusted entity that issues and signs digital certificates, binding public keys to identities
A server that stores encryption keys
A government agency that regulates cryptography
A protocol for secure key exchange
questions.explanation: A CA is a trusted third party that verifies identity and issues digital certificates. Root CAs are pre-installed in browsers/OSes; intermediate CAs issue end-entity certificates.

questions.question: What is the difference between CRL and OCSP for certificate validation?
answers: CRL is a downloaded list of revoked certs; OCSP provides real-time revocation status per certificate
CRL is faster; OCSP is more accurate
OCSP replaces certificates; CRL validates them
They are the same mechanism
questions.explanation: CRL (Certificate Revocation List) is a periodically published list of revoked certificates. OCSP (Online Certificate Status Protocol) provides real-time per-certificate revocation checking — faster and more current.

questions.question: What does Diffie-Hellman (DH) key exchange provide?
answers: A way for two parties to establish a shared secret over an insecure channel without transmitting the key
A method for encrypting email attachments
A digital signature algorithm
A symmetric encryption standard
questions.explanation: Diffie-Hellman allows two parties to derive a shared secret key over an untrusted channel. Neither party transmits the key itself — they each compute it independently using exchanged public values.

questions.question: Why is Elliptic Curve Cryptography (ECC) preferred for mobile devices?
answers: ECC provides equivalent security to RSA with much smaller key sizes, requiring less processing power
ECC is older and more tested than RSA
ECC uses symmetric keys for faster processing
ECC does not require a certificate authority
questions.explanation: ECC achieves the same security as RSA with significantly smaller keys (256-bit ECC ≈ 3072-bit RSA), making it ideal for constrained devices with limited CPU and battery resources.

questions.question: What does TLS (Transport Layer Security) protect?
answers: Data in transit between clients and servers (confidentiality, integrity, authentication)
Data stored in databases
Local file system encryption
User authentication credentials only
questions.explanation: TLS encrypts data in transit, authenticates the server (and optionally the client) via certificates, and ensures data integrity. It is the basis for HTTPS.

questions.question: What is certificate pinning?
answers: Hardcoding expected certificate or public key into an app to prevent MITM attacks
Physically securing a hardware security module
Renewing certificates automatically before expiry
Pinning a certificate to a specific IP address
questions.explanation: Certificate pinning embeds the expected server certificate or public key into an application, so the app rejects any certificate not matching — even if signed by a trusted CA.

questions.question: What is Perfect Forward Secrecy (PFS)?
answers: Using ephemeral session keys so past sessions cannot be decrypted if long-term keys are compromised
Encrypting all future communications with a permanent key
Ensuring no data is ever lost during transmission
Generating new passwords after each login
questions.explanation: PFS generates unique session keys for each session (using ephemeral DH). Even if the server's private key is later compromised, past sessions remain encrypted and cannot be decrypted.

questions.question: Why is quantum computing a threat to current asymmetric cryptography?
answers: Quantum computers can break RSA and ECC by solving the mathematical problems they rely on
Quantum computers can guess symmetric keys
Quantum computers make hashing reversible
Quantum computers can crack AES-256 in seconds
questions.explanation: Shor's algorithm on a quantum computer can efficiently factor large integers (breaking RSA) and solve discrete logarithms (breaking ECC/DH), motivating post-quantum cryptography research.
